mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Discovery 300Tdi engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 300Tdi engine typically costs £1,500–£2,500 supply only. Fitting adds £700–£1,300 at independent Land Rover specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Discovery 300Tdi engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the 300Tdi is widely regarded as one of Land Rover's most durable diesel engines. Its mechanical injection, cast-iron block, and simple design make it capable of 200,000+ miles. However, it requires strict timing belt discipline (every 60k miles or 5 years) and careful cooling system maintenance to avoid head gasket issues.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Discovery 300Tdi?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing belt failure (catastrophic if not replaced), head gasket failure (usually from overheating), and injection pump wear (hard starting, poor performance). All are well-documented and manageable with preventative maintenance.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Discovery 300Tdi engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Against a well-preserved Discovery 1 (values £5,000–£15,000 for good examples), a £2,000 reconditioned engine is 20–40% of the vehicle's value — often worth it. For a tired example with extensive rot, the Land Rover scrap threshold of 60% means evaluating body and chassis condition before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Discovery 300Tdi engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is stamped on the cylinder block, near the injection pump on the left-hand side. The 300Tdi was only fitted to Discovery 1 models from 1994 to 1998 (and Defender to 1999), so if your Discovery 1 is in that range and is a diesel, it's almost certainly a 300Tdi (the earlier 200Tdi was used 1989–1994). Check your V5C or VIN plate for confirmation.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the 300Tdi timing belt without doing a full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — preventative timing belt replacement is a routine service item costing £400–£700. It is not a repair, it's essential maintenance. If the belt snaps, you are usually looking at a replacement cylinder head or a full engine swap, so never delay this service.

replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 300Tdi engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£90/hr (independent Land Rover specialist). [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]

problems.title: Timing Belt Failure (Interference Engine)
problems.symptoms: Engine cuts out suddenly, no restart, loud bang from timing cover, bent valves — catastrophic if belt snaps.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if belt is overdue, stop driving and replace

problems.recommendation: The 300Tdi is an interference engine — if the timing belt breaks, pistons strike valves, often wrecking the cylinder head and sometimes the bottom end. Preventative belt replacement every 60,000 miles or 5 years (whichever comes first) is the single most important maintenance item. Many specialists recommend 48,000-mile intervals in heavy use. A £400–£700 belt change is peanuts compared to a £1,500+ engine replacement.

problems.title: Cylinder Head Gasket Failure
problems.symptoms: Pressurised cooling system, coolant loss, mayo in oil, white smoke from exhaust, overheating, loss of power.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if coolant is disappearing or overheating

problems.recommendation: The 300Tdi's cylinder head is its second most common failure point. Overheating (often from a failed thermostat, water pump, or radiator blockage) can warp the head and blow the gasket. Regular coolant changes and a functioning cooling system are essential. If you notice coolant loss or pressurisation, get a compression test and sniff test done immediately — catching it early avoids head damage.

problems.title: Injection Pump Wear (Bosch VE)
problems.symptoms: Hard starting (especially cold), rough idle, poor fuel economy, black smoke, loss of power, erratic engine speed.
problems.repairCost: £400–£900 (pump rebuild with new seals and internals) [PLACEHOLDER]

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can leave you stranded

problems.recommendation: The mechanical Bosch VE injection pump is robust but the internal governor and advance mechanism can wear, especially if fuel filtration is poor. Regular fuel filter changes and using good-quality diesel (or adding a lubricity additive) prolongs pump life. If starting becomes difficult or idle is uneven, have the pump timing checked before it fails completely.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Discovery 300Tdi? Check These First
buyIf: * Documented timing belt change within the last 40,000 miles or 3 years
* Cooling system in good order (recent water pump, thermostat, radiator)
* Full service history with regular oil changes (every 6,000 miles or yearly)
* No signs of coolant loss or pressurisation — cold and hot
avoidIf: * No proof of timing belt replacement — this is a dealbreaker
* Overheating history — warped head is common
* Milkshake in oil or coolant — head gasket failure
* Injection pump timing not recently checked — hard starting indicates wear

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 300Tdi is one of Land Rover's most beloved diesel engines — simple, mechanical, and famously tough. But it is not invincible: timing belt and head gasket are the two critical failure points. A well-maintained 300Tdi Discovery will easily exceed 200,000 miles; a neglected one will break your heart (and your wallet). Buy on history, not on low mileage alone.

cta.label: → Compare Discovery 300Tdi engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Discovery 300Tdi (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Timing Belt History — Proof of replacement within 60,000 miles or 5 years — non-negotiable
* Cold Start — Hard starting? That points to injection pump wear or timing
* Cooling System — Check for coolant loss, pressurisation, or overheating signs
* Smoke Check — Black smoke = overfuelling; white smoke = coolant in cylinders
* Oil Condition — Milky oil = head gasket; check dipstick and filler cap
* Fuel System — Fuel filter condition and lift pump operation (common failure)
* Test Drive — Any hesitation, misfire, or lack of power under load?
* MOT History — Look for emissions failures (often related to injection pump or injectors)
